critique.summary: Dune 3D is an open-source parametric 3D CAD application designed primarily for creating 3D-printed enclosures. It supports STEP import/export, fillets, chamfers, and uses Open CASCADE for its geometry kernel, with a constraint solver adapted from Solvespace.
mainPoints: Dune 3D aims to address user dissatisfaction with existing open-source 3D CAD tools like FreeCAD and Solvespace.
It supports parametric 3D modeling with STEP file compatibility, fillets, and chamfers using the Open CASCADE kernel.
The application reuses components from Horizon EDA, including the 3D viewport and interactive manipulator.
The constraint solver is derived from Solvespace's solver but patched for performance and feature completeness.
Dune 3D is cross-platform with build instructions available for Windows and macOS.
The project is hosted on GitHub with active development and community discussion platforms.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Dune 3D's features and motivations are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository. The use of Open CASCADE for STEP support and fillets/chamfers is accurate, as is the adaptation of Solvespace's constraint solver. The repository shows active development, multiple contributors, and documentation supporting the described features. There is no evidence contradicting the stated motivations or technical details.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party reviews or comparisons with other CAD software beyond the author's personal experience.
Performance benchmarks or user feedback on usability and stability are not discussed.
The long-term maintenance and community support level beyond the initial development phase are not addressed.
Potential limitations or missing features compared to established CAD software are not detailed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the new Dune3D parametric 3D CAD application, with users comparing it to existing tools like SolveSpace and FreeCAD, expressing interest in its features and usability, and sharing related open-source projects. Some comments highlight the software's simplicity and potential, while others question its longevity and uniqueness.

comments.author: Evidlo
comments.text:
Some other open source 3D CAD tools:

Code-based

- CadQuery - https://github.com/CadQuery/cadquery/

- build123d - https://github.com/gumyr/build123d

- OpenSCAD - https://openscad.cloud/openscad/

GUI (browser-based)

- Cadmium (abandoned, cool idea) - https://mattferraro.dev/posts/cadmium
comments.analysis: Provides a well-informed list of alternative open-source 3D CAD tools, adding valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: freeopinion
comments.text: Dune3d comes off like a reskin of SolveSpace. SolveSpace is pretty awesome, so that's not a knock, per se. I'll leave it to somebody with more experience to fill in what value Dune3D adds beyond SolveSpace.
comments.analysis: Offers a comparative perspective based on experience, suggesting Dune3D's similarity to an established tool, which is a useful critique.

comments.author: nathanmcrae
comments.text:
I have used this for throwing together some models for 3D printing. I've found it very intuitive, though I'm not sure how ergonomic it would be for complex assemblies.

I really like the space-key based command access and default shortcuts for all the commands.
comments.analysis: Shares first-hand user experience highlighting usability strengths and potential limitations, contributing practical insight.

comments.author: Levitating
comments.text:
Another simple GTK4 app for the ecosystem, nice.

In case anyone is wondering, Dune3D as a flatpak is about 33mb. FreeCAD is 354mb. I enjoy having simple solutions that get simple things done. Will definitely give Dune3D a try.
comments.analysis: Provides useful technical details and a positive viewpoint on Dune3D's simplicity and size compared to FreeCAD.

comments.author: fallat
comments.text: So this is a smudge of like 4 projects? Huh. Definitely interested, but I wonder about the longevity of the system. That's one thing about the code cad systems I like: it's pretty easy to port code from one to another.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the project's sustainability and integration of multiple codebases.

comments.author: jetter
comments.text: Also check out modelrift.com which is based on openscad foundation. See the dynamic customizer which allows to edit any model parameter, re-render and get .stl: https://modelrift.com/models/customizable-liquid-funnel - it works _completely_ in your browser by using WASM
comments.analysis: Shares a relevant related project with useful features, enhancing the discussion with additional resources.

comments.author: idiotsecant
comments.text: Its not often you see 'fillets and chamfers' are tip-line features in the readme for CAD packages. But good on you for building something.
comments.analysis: Highlights a specific feature uncommon in CAD readmes, showing appreciation but without deep technical analysis.

comments.author: mountainriver
comments.text: FreeCAD is the worst. Thanks for building this!
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong negative opinion about FreeCAD without constructive reasoning, reducing its usefulness.

comments.author: raziel2701
comments.text: Would love to see a quick video demo showcasing the features, look and feel of the software. The same team made horizon eda, and I wasn't able to find videos on youtube about it that were newer than 2022, so I never gave it a shot either.
comments.analysis: Requests additional media to better evaluate the software, a reasonable but non-informative comment.

comments.author: reconnecting
comments.text: Without context, it sounds like an FPS adaptation of a Dune game.
comments.analysis: Makes a humorous but irrelevant remark that does not contribute to the technical discussion.
